title: Randy Brogdon: Righteous Indignation in Response to New York's New Law
author: Randy Brogdon
orientation: Former Oklahoma state senator Randy Brogdon reacts to New York's January 2019 Reproductive Health Act by arguing that Roe v. Wade is not 'the law of the land,' that the Pro-Life movement has unintentionally entrenched abortion through incremental regulation, and that Oklahoma should pass Sen. Joseph Silk's SB-13 to abolish abortion outright.
audiences: Pro-life Christians comfortable with incremental restrictions
Oklahoma legislators and voters weighing SB-13
Pastors and church leaders silent on abortion
Skeptics who treat Roe v. Wade as binding law

questions.question: What did New York's new abortion law actually do, according to Brogdon?
questions.audience: Skeptics who treat Roe v. Wade as binding law
questions.answer: Brogdon describes the law as codifying abortion in the New York Constitution, permitting abortion up to the due date, allowing non-doctors to perform abortions, and stripping protection from babies born alive during botched abortions. He frames it as the unmasking of what the abortion movement has always wanted.
questions.quote: The bill is codified in the New York Constitution and allows for non-doctors to perform the abortion. It removes protection from a baby that was accidentally born during a botched abortion.  A precious little baby can now be legally murdered after birth in the State of New York.

questions.question: Why does Brogdon say Roe v. Wade is not 'the law of the land'?
questions.audience: Skeptics who treat Roe v. Wade as binding law
questions.answer: He argues that laws can only be made through legislation by consent of the governed, and that consent cannot be given to immoral acts. A court opinion that violates the moral law and bypasses the legislature therefore lacks the character of law and should never be referred to as such by people who want to abolish abortion.
questions.quote: Laws cannot be made by a court opinion, (only through legislation) nor can a legislature make an unjust or immoral law. So, it stands beyond reason and any doubt that Roe v Wade is not the “law of the land” (neither is New York’s new law) rather it is a subversive, immoral, and unjust assault on life and liberty.

questions.question: How does Brogdon implicate the Pro-Life movement in New York's new law?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians comfortable with incremental restrictions
questions.answer: He claims pro-abortionists could not have advanced this far without the 'latent help' of pro-lifers, who adopted the phrase 'law of the land,' settled into a defensive posture, and championed legislation that regulated rather than abolished abortion, effectively codifying it in state law.
questions.quote: The pro-abortionists would have not gotten this bill passed in New York without the latent help of the Pro-Life movement.  Please let me explain before you get mad.

questions.question: What specific incremental measures does Brogdon condemn as compromise?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians comfortable with incremental restrictions
questions.answer: He lists ultrasound requirements, third-trimester limits, heartbeat bills, pain-capable bills, and twenty-week bans as examples of pro-life lobbying in Oklahoma that allow abortion under conditions rather than abolishing it, calling all of them offensive to God.
questions.quote: How can any Christian believe that God is pleased when we support incremental legislation that allows for abortion IF, the mom has an ultrasound before the baby is murdered, IF, the abortion is performed before the third trimester, IF, an abortion is done before a heartbeat is detected, IF, an abortion is performed before a baby can feel pain, IF, the abortion is performed before twenty weeks?

questions.question: What could states have done differently after Roe, in Brogdon's view?
questions.audience: Oklahoma legislators and voters weighing SB-13
questions.answer: He argues that even one state legislature and governor exercising their constitutional and moral authority to defy the Roe opinion could have saved the 65 million babies killed since 1973. The failure was political and moral cowardice, not legal impossibility.
questions.quote: Had even one State legislature and governor exercised their Constitutional authority, and more importantly their moral authority, to defy an unjust court opinion, 65,000,000 babies could have been saved.  But they didn’t, and now our babies are dead.

questions.question: What does Brogdon say is the number one duty of government?
questions.audience: Skeptics who treat Roe v. Wade as binding law
questions.answer: He grounds civil authority in the Declaration's principle that everyone is created with the right to life, derives just government power from the consent of the governed, and concludes that protecting innocent life is government's primary obligation.
questions.quote: We have forgotten the cornerstone of our Country’s founding principle, is that everyone is created with the “right to life.”  Since 1973, Oklahoma, and the rest of the country have ignored an important and fundamental principle; that Government was instituted by us, and given its just power, by our consent, and its number one duty is to protect innocent life.

questions.question: What does Brogdon say about the silence of pastors and priests?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders silent on abortion
questions.answer: Writing one week after the New York bill passed, he asks readers whether their pastors mentioned it from the pulpit, and indicts the church's continuing silence while fifteen Oklahoma babies are aborted each day.
questions.quote: I write this article one week after the evil men and women in the New York legislature passed the baby killer bill.  Did your pastor or priest mention anything from the pulpit?  The church remains silent and 15 preborn babies will be murdered in Oklahoma today!

questions.question: What concrete legislative action does Brogdon ask Oklahomans to support?
questions.audience: Oklahoma legislators and voters weighing SB-13
questions.answer: He points to SB-13, authored by Sen. Silk and pending before the Oklahoma Senate in February 2019, as the vehicle for abolishing abortion in Oklahoma, and notes that a new Republican governor and legislature give abolitionists every needed tool.
questions.quote: Oklahoma has a wonderful opportunity to Abolish Abortion with SB-13 by Sen. Silk, which is before the State Senate this year, (February 2019).

questions.question: What hope does Brogdon offer women who have had abortions?
questions.answer: He explicitly states his words are not meant to demean or condemn but to give hope, assuring post-abortive women that God forgives and that His forgiveness is available to them.
questions.quote: My words are to give hope to those who may have had an abortion and ensure you that God forgives and His forgiveness can be yours.

questions.question: What false strategy does Brogdon say pro-lifers settled on instead of state action?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians comfortable with incremental restrictions
questions.answer: He criticizes the strategy of waiting for the Supreme Court to overrule itself by adding more conservative justices, calling it a 'red herring' that distracted the movement from authoring legislation to actually abolish abortion.
questions.quote: The Pro-Life agenda decided to wait on the Supreme Court to overrule themselves.  They settled on a red hearing idea that we just need a couple of more Conservatives on the Court.  Another big mistake!

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Direct follow-up on the same Oklahoma SB-13 abolition bill Brogdon urges readers to support.

related.title: Watch - Of Giants & Heroes: Joseph Silk
related.reason: Profile of Sen. Joseph Silk, the SB-13 author Brogdon names as the bill's sponsor.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Systematic version of Brogdon's central critique that incremental regulation entrenches rather than ends abortion.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Defends the constitutional argument Brogdon invokes that states may and should defy unjust federal court opinions.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Engages the same legislator-defiance question Brogdon raises about state authority over federal court opinions.

related.title: "I am a pro-life Oklahoman who voted to table a bill that would abolish all abortion"
related.reason: Documents the kind of pro-life Oklahoma legislator resistance Brogdon warned would defeat abolition efforts.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
related.reason: Later answer to Brogdon's complaint about pulpit silence — Oklahoma Baptists eventually passing an abolitionist resolution.
